---
title: "Mass-dependent fractionation"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Mass-dependent fractionation}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
bibliography: "/home/kd/PARA/Notes/My Library.bib"
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

```{r setup}
library(liaendmembers)
```

This method is adapted from the provenance algorithm identified by @A.D.B+24, which was originally tested on lead isotope ratios within ancient Greek coinage. The algorithm accounts for both natural and analytical isotope fractionation. It proposes a statistical measure to determine the distances between the lead isotope compositions of ores and artifacts. Finally, this measure allows for statistical testing at any specified confidence level.

## Stadandared Euclidean distance

The standard model for estimating three-dimensional distances for lead isotope values involves measuring the Euclidean distance between two vectors, $\vec{x}$ and $\vec{x}'$. This calculation follows the established formula:$$d(\vec{x}, \vec{x}') = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - x'_i)^2}$$

This is the method used by the function `isoprov_dist()` when the `dist_type` value is set to `"ed"` which stands for 'Eucilidian distance'.

According to @A.D.B+24, the standard method fails to account for statistical errors. These errors are addressed by weighting the data with the full covariance matrix $W$ of the NIST 981 lead isotope reference material. The adjusted distance is calculated as:$$d = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}')^T W^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}')}$$In this formula, $T$ denotes the transpose of the vector.

## Algorisum acounting for Mass-dependant fractionation

The methodology by @A.D.B+24 utilizes the distance $d_i$ between an artifact and the mass-dependent fractionation line passing through ore $i$. This is implemented by setting the dist_type parameter to "mfd". Provided lead isotope ratios differ by less than one decimal point, respective fractionation lines are considered parallel. The distance $d_i$ is computed using the vector $\vec{\Delta X_i} = (\vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_0)$, where $\vec{x}_0$ represents the artifact and $\vec{x}_i$ represents the ore.The artifact is represented as:$$\vec{x}_0=\left[\left(^{206}Pb/^{204}Pb\right)_0,\left(^{207}Pb/^{204}Pb\right)_0,\left(^{208}Pb/^{204}Pb\right)_0\right]$$The ore is represented as:$$\vec{x}_i=\left[\left(^{206}Pb/^{204}Pb\right)_i,\left(^{207}Pb/^{204}Pb\right)_i,\left(^{208}Pb/^{204}Pb\right)_i\right]$$The mass-dependent fractionation line is defined by the vector $\vec{v}$:$$\vec{v}=\left[2\left(^{206}Pb/^{204}Pb\right)_0,3\left(^{207}Pb/^{204}Pb\right)_0,4\left(^{208}Pb/^{204}Pb\right)_0\right]$$The digits in $\vec{v}$ correspond to the atomic mass differences of the isotopes. With $\vec{n} = \vec{v}/||\vec{v}||$ as the unit vector, distance $d_i$ is calculated via the cross-product:$$d_i = ||\vec{\Delta X_i} \times \vec{n}||$$The fractionation factor $f_i$ is computed as a dot product:$$f_i = \vec{\Delta X_i} \cdot \vec{n}$$To obtain a dimensionless distance, the error matrix $W$ is defined as:$$W=
\begin{bmatrix}
2x^*_x & 0 & 0\\
0 & 3x^*_y & 0\\
0 & 0 & 4x^*_z\\
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0.96 & 0.94\\
0.96 & 1 & 0.96\\
0.94 & 0.96 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
2x^*_x & 0 & 0\\
0 & 3x^*_y & 0\\
0 & 0 & 4x^*_z\\
\end{bmatrix}$$The outer matrices are diagonal standard deviation matrices for common lead, with a fractionation factor $s$ of 0.001. Squared distances $|d|^2$ are normalized to the two-dimensional shadow of $W$ perpendicular to $\vec{n}$. This requires Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization to find perpendicular vectors. Since $W$ is derived from reference materials, this metric is not a formal Mahalanobis distance. Normalized squared distances follow a chi-squared distribution with two degrees of freedom. Consequently, ores with $|d|^2$ values exceeding 5.99 are rejected at the 95% confidence level.
